Our Indian aluminium operations are integrated and consist of bauxite mining, alumina refining, smelting and converting primary metal into value-added products. We have dedicated sources for critical raw materials such as bauxite, power and coal. Our finished products include alumina, primary aluminium in the form of ingots, billets and wire rods, value-added products such as rolled products, extrusions and foils. Metallurgical alumina is used for our own captive needs. Chemical alumina and hydrates are used in range of industries including water treatment, fillers in cables and plastics, refractories and ceramics, glass among others.

Hindalco produces LME grade copper cathodes, continuous cast copper rods in various sizes, and precious metals like gold and silver. Hindalco is one of the major manufacturers of 19.6mm diameter copper rods, which are used for railway electrification. The co-product, sulphuric acid, is partly utilised to produce phosphoric acid and fertilisers like di-ammonium phosphate (DAP).

• Significant contribution to emissions due to 
continued reliance on coal in aluminium and 
copper operations.
• Heavy dependence on fossil fuels limits 
ability to transition to low carbon energy.
− It increases exposure to regulatory 
penalties, vulnerability to carbon pricing, 
stringent environmental regulations, 
and EU’s Carbon Border Adjustment 
Mechanism (CBAM).
− Threatens long-term cost efficiency and 
market competitiveness in an economy 
transitioning to low-carbon energy.

• Expanding renewable energy 
capacity. 
• Optimising energy use, investing in 
low carbon technologies to reduce 
our reliance on fossil fuels.
• Collaborating with upstream partners 
and aligning with evolving climate 
regulations, as we support our Net 
Zero 2050 target while enhancing 
operational efficiency and resilience 
across our value chain.

• Our aluminium and copper operations, including those under Novelis, generate complex waste streams such as bauxite residue, spent pot lining, vanadium sludge, and salt cake. These materials pose risks to land, water, air, and human health. • With increasing land constraints and rising public scrutiny, inadequate waste management could result in environmental degradation, reputational damage, and regulatory consequences

To strengthen waste management practices, we committed ₹448.78 crore in operational improvements and ₹30.73 lakh in related R&D during FY 2024-25. • We have committed to achieving Zero Waste to Landfill by 2050.

• Our operations in water-scarce regions such 
as Dahej, Belagavi, Asoj, and Kuppam are 
increasingly exposed to the risks of water 
resource depletion.
• These conditions may lead to elevated 
operational costs, stricter regulatory 
controls, and potential community tensions 
due to limited water availability. Prolonged 
operations under such constraints could 
jeopardise environmental compliance 
and may threaten the long-term viability of 
production at these sites

• To address water scarcity risks, 
we are implementing rainwater 
harvesting, recycling, and reuse 
systems across our operations.
• We have defined targets to achieve 
water positivity across mining and 
downstream operations by 2025. ZLD 
goals are set for the Aluminium business 
by FY 2025–26 and the Copper 
business by FY 2029–30, supporting 
long-term sustainability and regulatory 
compliance in water-stressed regions.

• Non-compliance with air emission standards 
particularly concerning SOx, NOx, 
particulate matter (PM), and fluoride poses 
significant risks to our operations.
• Imposition of regulatory penalties across 
various geographies may lead to increased 
scrutiny from both authorities and local 
communities.
• These may disrupt operations, lead to 
delayed permits and damage our reputation 
with stakeholders, thus becoming threats to 
our sustainability.

•To mitigate regulatory, reputational, 
and operational risks, we are actively 
managing emissions through the 
deployment of advanced control 
technologies, continuous monitoring 
systems, and proactive maintenance 
practices.
• Our efforts are aimed at ensuring 
compliance, protecting ecosystems, 
and supporting sustainable industrial 
operations

• Mining activities in ecologically sensitive 
regions may lead to more protests from 
community and more scrutiny during public 
hearings.
• Allegations regarding biodiversity 
degradation and potential non-compliance 
with conservation regulations may result 
in damage to reputation and delay the 
acquisition of critical environmental 
clearances, posing risks to regulatory 
approvals, stakeholder acceptance, and 
business continuity.

• We have developed Biodiversity 
Management Plans (BMP) for 28 
high- and medium-priority sites, with 
plans to expand coverage by FY 
2024–25.
• Proactive land use management
ensures responsible site development 
and minimises ecological disruption.
• Habitat restoration efforts are 
underway to rehabilitate disturbed 
ecosystems and strengthen 
community trust.

Safety incidents, reflected in our Lost 
Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR), pose 
significant risks to employee well-being, 
operational continuity and regulatory 
compliance. Such incidents can lead to 
production disruptions, reputational damage, 
and potential penalties.

• As part of our commitment to Zero 
Harm, we undertook key mitigating 
actions in FY 2024-25 to strengthen 
workplace safety and risk management. 
We achieved a 5% reduction in our 
Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate 
(LTIFR) by enhancing safety protocols, 
surpassing safety observation targets, 
and investing 1.7 million man-hours in 
safety trainings, cross-entity audits, and 
digital tool integration. 
• These measures enabled us to 
proactively mitigate safety risks, ensure 
regulatory compliance, and reinforce a 
strong safety culture that supports our 
long-term sustainability objectives.
• Active contribution for the upliftment of the underserved communities lifts the burden of poverty and aids in inclusive growth, in alignment with the UNSDGs.

We contribute significantly to regional and national development through employment generation, tax contributions, infrastructure investments, and foreign exchange earnings from aluminium exports. • By investing in skill development, local procurement, and community initiatives, we foster economic resilience and shared value. • Additionally, rising global demand for sustainable aluminium presents a strategic opportunity to expand our presence in eco-conscious markets and strengthen our leadership in the green materials sector.

Due to our reliance on a limited group of copper concentrate suppliers, we face higher risks related to supply chain disruptions and supplier non-compliance. • Environmental and social challenges in these areas such as water stress, deforestation, and community tensions can impact supply continuity, escalate costs, and affect operational efficiency.

To mitigate supply chain risks, we focus on sustainability, resilience, and transparency in our supply chain strategy. We enforce ESG compliance, promote ethical sourcing, and engage local suppliers to minimise environmental and social impacts. • Through supplier diversification, continuous monitoring, and operational efficiency, we ensure business continuity, cost control, and alignment with global sustainability goals
Circular Economy and End-of Life  Recycling

Circularity efforts, driven by Novelis' advanced
recycling initiatives, significantly reduce reliance on
virgin raw materials, lower energy consumption, and
cut greenhouse gas emissions.
• Contribute to environmental sustainability while
generating green employment opportunities,
strengthening supply chain accountability, and enhancing
community well-being by minimising industrial pollution
and promoting ethical sourcing practices.

Improper management of bauxite residue can lead to spillage, posing serious environmental and operational risks. Such events can disrupt business continuity, erode stakeholders’ confidence, and threaten license to operate.

We introduced a Tailings
Management Policy aligned with
the Global Industry Standard on
Tailings Management (GISTM), and conducted risk assessments on all sites. We found that 50% of active facilities have risk potential, which lead to the strengthening of our
oversight and control measures.

Absence of adequate measures for mine closure may pose regulatory and reputational risks. Mine closure process must comply with host of laws. Failure to meet these
requirements can lead to regulatory scrutiny and reputational damage. Further, closure involves significant costs such as, decommissioning, land restoration, and community rehabilitation. If not planned early, these costs can escalate and affect financial performance

We recognise land as a critical mining resource. We have implemented a Sustainable Land Use Framework to manage land transformation across the mine lifecycle. The framework emphasises conservation, restoration, rehabilitation, resettlement, and responsible mine closure.  We have also identified risks that may arise during the closure and adopted mitigation actions to minimise them.
Embed DEI into our core strategy, we can unlock long-term value not just for workforce, but for business and the communities we serve.

We contribute significantly to regional and national development through employment generation, tax contributions, infrastructure investments, and foreign exchange earnings
from aluminium exports. • By investing in skill development, local procurement, and community initiatives, we foster economic resilience and shared value. • Additionally, rising global demand for sustainable aluminium presents a strategic opportunity to expand our presence in eco-conscious markets and strengthen our leadership in the green materials sector

Inadequate data protection measures, employee resistance to technology adoption, and targeted cyberattacks on sensitive information pose serious threats. These
include the risk of data breaches, system disruptions, financial losses, regulatory penalties, reputational damage, and erosion of customer trust

• To mitigate cybersecurity and operational risks, we are investing in robust IT infrastructure and comprehensive risk management strategies.
• These efforts are designed to safeguard our operations, enhance digital resilience, and reinforce our position as a technology leader in the metals and mining sector.

With the increasing stringency of ESG regulations globally, non-compliance with evolving disclosure standards poses significant regulatory and reputational risks.
Failure to meet these expectations can lead to penalties, investor scrutiny, and erosion of market credibility.

We have been disclosing our ESG performance through our Integrated Report which is published annually. We also participate in ESG assessments such as CDP, S&P Global
Corporate Sustainability Assessment, benchmarking our performance on ESG parameters.

Our employees are  an integral part of  our journey towards  transformational, responsible and sustainable change. We engage with the employees on a continous basis. Some of the key concerns and expectations from employees include fair wages and equal opportunities, training and skill development with career growth, rewards and recognition, employee wellbeing, occupational health and safety, transparent communication, medical facilities and operational continuity.

Customers drive sales and are key to process improvements, quality enhancement, service performance and cost optimisation. Hence, our business cannot continue to exist without them. We engage with the customers on a continous basis. Some of the key topics and concerns raised during engagement include post-sales support, chinese and local competition, sustainable products offerings and solutions, enhancing our product portfolio, NPS score, health and safety.

Suppliers are the brand ambassador of our products. They play an indispensable role in the product life cycle.We engage with the suppliers and vendors on a continous basis. Some of the key topics and concerns raised during engagement include continuity of orders, capacity building, pricing and negotiation and embedded sustainability in supply chain, ESG assessment of suppliers, Contractor safetly management.

Government and regulatory bodies promote and regulate our actions, ensuring business continuity through compliance with laws. We enagage with the them as and when required and some of the key topics on which engagement is carried out include tax and royalties, pollution prevention, community development, regulatory compliance, and green economy.

Community engagement enhances our operational efficiency and positive outlook as responsible corporate. Continous community engagement fosters a positive workplace culture and values. The key topics and concerns raised during the engagement include infrastructure development, training and livelihood programmes, local employment, socioeconomic assessment, responsible mining, participation in social services and community relation through volunteering.

Investors and shareholders provides financial resources to compound the business growth. The key concerns and expectations of the shareholders include sustainable growth and returns, cost optimisation, enterprise risk management, market share, ESG commitment and disclosure, operational and financial performance, risk management, corporate governance and  ethics and integrity.

Industry associations have a significant impact on corporate sustainability and offers in-depth advisory on industry trends. These are the route to interact with the industry and the government. We engage with the industry associations periodically and the key concerns and expectations include regulations, compliance with industry standards, policy advocacy efforts and business collaborations.

Media helps in projecting our vision and strategy to the various stakeholders and, hence, drives growth. We engage with th media periodically and the key concerns and expectations include digital platforms such as social media and websites, press releases and interviews.
